critic_raw:
Including hospital identifiers as a feature to 'mitigate confounds' from hospital-specific policies is methodologically backwards. Hospital identity is a proxy for the confounding factors (resource availability, policy differences, patient population composition). Including it as a feature teaches the model to route predictions by hospital identity, which is appropriate during evaluation but would require hospital identity at prediction time in deployment — and more critically, it means the model learns hospital-specific readmission risk profiles rather than generalizable clinical patterns. The performance on the test set, which includes known hospitals, will overestimate performance on new hospitals.

Recall as the primary metric without a precision constraint is operationally meaningless. Recall is maximized at 1.0 by predicting every patient as a readmission risk. The design uses recall for model selection but does not specify the precision constraint, recall operating point, or outreach capacity assumption that makes a specific recall level meaningful. Different models can trivially achieve equivalent recall at very different precisions.

The stratified random split 'assumes temporal autocorrelation is sufficiently weak.' For a readmission prediction model trained on a hospital with evolving discharge protocols, care management programs, and seasonal census fluctuations, this assumption is not verified. The design does not test whether temporal structure exists before committing to the random split.

The attention mechanism is framed as the key differentiator of the RNN architecture over logistic regression, but no ablation is proposed to verify whether the attention actually attends to clinically meaningful events versus simply learning which visit positions correlate with the label regardless of clinical content.
all_issues_raised: Including hospital identifiers as features to control for confounds teaches the model hospital-specific readmission risk profiles — performance on the test set (known hospitals) will overestimate performance on new hospitals, and the model becomes a hospital-lookup table rather than a generalizable clinical predictor.

Recall as the primary model selection metric without a precision constraint or outreach capacity specification is undefined — recall of 1.0 is trivially achievable by flagging all patients, making recall alone an unworkable selection criterion.

The 'sufficiently weak temporal autocorrelation' assumption for hospital EHR data is asserted without verification — care management program launches, seasonal census changes, and discharge protocol updates create temporal structure that the design makes no attempt to detect before committing to a random split.

The attention mechanism is claimed to capture sequential interactions but no ablation is proposed to verify whether attention learns clinically meaningful event patterns or simply learns position-based correlates of the outcome label.
all_issues_adjudicated: Including hospital identifiers as features to control for confounds teaches the model hospital-specific readmission risk profiles — performance on the test set (known hospitals) will overestimate performance on new hospitals, and the model becomes a hospital-lookup table rather than a generalizable clinical predictor.

Recall as the primary model selection metric without a precision constraint or outreach capacity specification is undefined — recall of 1.0 is trivially achievable by flagging all patients, making recall alone an unworkable selection criterion.

The 'sufficiently weak temporal autocorrelation' assumption for hospital EHR data is asserted without verification — care management program launches, seasonal census changes, and discharge protocol updates create temporal structure that the design makes no attempt to detect before committing to a random split.

The attention mechanism is claimed to capture sequential interactions but no ablation is proposed to verify whether attention learns clinically meaningful event patterns or simply learns position-based correlates of the outcome label.
